sentences.text: Yeah.
I left them without saying goodbye.
And I ran out from the house.
I couldn't see my father and how he was looking.
He was terrible, terrible looking, and crying like a small child.
And that's all what I-- I left them.
And then in the camp-- and I was-- I heard that they were all gathered in one place and then sent to-- into wagons-- cattle wagons-- on a train-- away.
We did not know where to.
After awhile, we found out that that was Treblinka.
When we heard it, we all cried terribly.
But we had to go onto work.
And that's Skarzysko HASAG.
We couldn't stay home and cry.
So-- but our days was terribly dark.
They were terribly dark days we had-- cried terribly.
And everybody was so upset.
And the-- the Germans even noticed what happened.
So some kids said to them, why?
And where was-- there was one German which was different than the others.
What was his name?
And he was a good German.
I don't remember his name.
But he said to us children-- just like a father-- children don't cry.
And don't show off the Germans that you are crying.
Don't do that.
Keep on living.
You will survive.
And that's what-- he was a good man.
But he couldn't do anything either.
Both group-- what could he do amongst all these beasts, even when he-- if he was good?
And that's how we went on.
We-- [SIGHS] --we took that for granted.
When you left for Skarzysko, were you allowed to take any of your belongings with you?
Yes.
I took a-- a bag with me.
I took a bag.
It was the more necessary things-- not big-- a small bag.
Then I remember we said to my-- our meister that we didn't take all kinds of clothes for us.
So they allowed my sisters to go home and to bring some more.
They had that privilege.
So they brought some of-- more of our clothes.
It was middle of summer and during the day.
Yeah.
And they brought us now to another camp where my sister were.
My sisters were in a different camp, Werk B.
And we came to Werk A.
And this was a better place to work-- in Werk A.
But I didn't want to be in Werk A.
And I-- [SIGHS] --and I went to one of the Germans who was there and begged him.
I would like to-- that I had my sister in Werk B.
And I would like to be together with them.
So he allowed me.
He said, OK, I'll take you to Werk B.
Yeah, I had been in contact with them when they came home.
They told us where they work.
And I-- when I came over there, I saw-- they told us where we were-- that we are not in Werk B.
We are Werk A.
This was a ammunition-- ammunition [NON-ENGLISH]-- ammunition factory.
And it was called HASAG, after a German company.
And over there, we made the bullets.
Where-- where I was, we made bullets.
In another camp, not far away, they made the-- the powder for the bullets.
And we-- and there another camp was where they made big, big cannons.
They were working where-- there were people working there already for-- for years-- for a-- for-- from 1940, because--
They were very bad condition.
And when I came into-- with my sisters, they selected me to be a forelady.
What?
No.
No, it wasn't.
When I arrived, there was no selection.
They were just taking the new people and-- and giving them to-- the work what they have to do.
Yeah.
A separate camp.
The conditions Werk B were worse than Werk A.
And Werk C was worse than Werk B-- that was a death camp.
The people from the powder, what they made for the ammunition, became very yellow-- yellow like terrible looking.
And all were like [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was a horrible camp.
It was horrible meisters-- horrible.
They-- they got such beatings-- terrible, I heard.
When I saw these people-- when I worked already from Werk B, which they send us to Werk A.
And the-- these people came to the-- to the showers.
They didn't have showers there.
And here were showers.
When they came to the showers, I can't-- I couldn't look at them-- how they looked-- just like [NON-ENGLISH]-- yellow, not-- dressed in shabby, old clothes.
It was a horrible sight to see.
The number?
I don't remember.
I stayed with my sisters in one barrack.
And this was a barrack for foreladies.
Not in our camp-- no-- a kommandanten.
German-- German term, yeah, yeah.
Kommandanten.
Kapo?
In our place, they didn't call it kapo-- kommandanten.
I was a kommandanten.
My sisters were kommandanten, all three of were, because we were young, still having our clothes from home, and looking not bad.
And that's what they want.
And I pleaded with him.
I don't want to be a kommandanten, because I knew what it-- is involved with-- to-- to-- to look and-- and to-- to say to somebody how to work and what to do.
And I didn't like it.
I wanted to be a plain worker.
But they didn't hear to my pleas.
And that what I was.
Certain things I had to do is to ladle out the food for them, and the-- the bread, and see that everything they work, and be like a angel-- a guardian angel for them.
That's what I was.
But the others were different.
For instance, where they work, they gave us sticks to beat them when they don't work.
And we always were two-- two kommandantens in-- on one shift.
The other one was one.
And then I had one.
I never touched a person.
And she did.
Yes-- Bela Sperling.
And when the-- but I watched out for the meister.
Through the window, if he-- I saw him on the side.
I just winked to my-- to the people-- to the coworkers that work there that he's coming.
And they knew it.
So they started working.
And everything was good.
One day he came in, the meister, and he saw a man washing his hands.
He called me up and said, Paula, how come that man is washing their hands?
Why didn't you use the stick?
And I said, I don't know.
How I did get these words in my mouth, how I responded to him-- but somebody was from-- from up in heaven or a guardian angel over me-- how I spoke to it-- to them.
And he understood.
I said, Herr-- Herr Meister, in German.
Shall I say?
Herr Meister, I don't [GERMAN].
And he was-- he was a smart man.
He liked one Jew to Jew-- to hit the other.
He liked that-- seeing that.
But he didn't like-- really, he didn't-- he didn't like when he saw that.
He understood that I had some dignity.
And I don't want to do it.
He knew it.
And he had the respect for me.
Never did he say one bad word to me, where he joked as the other one.
And she was like, too-- too chummy with him.
He had-- for her he didn't have respect at all.
He-- he told her dirty jokes, and this-- and too chummy-- too chummy with her.
And I was very strict, and answered to the point, and begged him if I had to.
And yes-- about the other incident with that man-- I said, that man is not-- that's not his fault.
I knew he was going to wash his hands.
That was my fault, because he didn't feel good.
That's what I saw-- I told him.
So for that, he punished me.
I had to wash the floors in the [NON-ENGLISH] after work.
So I did.
That was one incident.
In the--
Herring.
This was-- he was known for a very bad guy.
But he was a smart guy-- a very bad and strict man.
And there were other incident in the camp which I don't like.
I didn't like it.
The director, Glower, once come over-- came over to me.
And he said, Paula, I would like you to-- to bring me some information from the people from camp.
You know, I don't mean from the Jews, from the Poles.
I said, Herr-- Herr Director, I can't do it, because I don't know what's going on in the camp.
I'm doing my business.
I'm keeping order during-- by the girls.
And everything goes smoothly here.
And I don't have any other interests to know.
Oh, so you don't want to tell me anything?
I said, I don't know.
So he said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- so we'll see.
OK.
And I went home and to-- to camp-- to the barracks.
And I just said to my sisters-- I told them the whole story.
And in case I will not-- something will happen, you'll know why.
And I was afraid that he can send me-- take me out at night and shoot me or something.
But I didn't want-- I'm not going to do it.
But it was gone.
Nothing happened.
The other time, there were-- a few months later, he came over again, after me, because I was not going in the line with-- with the-- to work in-- I was out of line seeing that they go.
He came over again.
And he said to me very quietly-- and I said, no Herr Direktor, I cannot do it.
You can do whatever you want.
Everything is in-- in your power.
And I cannot do it.
And so he said, I'll send your sisters to [NON-ENGLISH].
I'll send one of your sisters to [NON-ENGLISH].
[SIGHS]
I see.
What can I say?
And the same thing-- I came.
And I talked to one of the-- of the kommandantens there to the-- from the camp.
And he was the head of the camp.
And I told him-- so and so, so and so.
That's the second time he approached me.
And something happened.
I want you to-- to do something about it.
And I came to the barracks, told the sisters-- nothing happened.
He didn't do anything to me.
I with my sisters were in one-- the same barrack.
But there were tens of barracks in the same camp.
Yeah.
There was.
But nothing went--
No, the same food-- the same food.
But when-- when I ladled out the food, I tried to take it from the bottom.
Every-- each time I take the ladle, I took it from the bottom, because everything's set on the bottom.
And on top was just plain water with-- with parsnips or something, or horse meat.
[SIGHS]
So the girls stand in the line to get the-- they had their-- their [NON-ENGLISH].
What do you call it?
Their-- they get it for the-- for the soup.
So I gave them out.
And when I was finished, if I had some over, I-- I saw the girls were very hungry-- and the men especially.
So I called them in to give them the rest-- whatever it was left over.
And of course, maybe I had another soup too.
So that's what I had from that ladle job.
But I knew about very bad things about the other kommandantens, what they did to it.
They-- I don't want to say it.
But unfortunately, my heart was breaking when I saw it.
They gave the first one the soup.
They didn't even mix it out.
They gave it from the top.
And the leftover was the-- all the other things in the bottom.
And they sold it, unfortunately.
They sold it to people.
They paid them money.
Or to every kommandant or kommandanten had a-- a person who did for them the chores.
So they gave them to them for that.
And they cooked for them.
There were a fire outside.
And they cooked for them beautiful soups, like at home.
I never had that in my life.
And I never did sell a soup.
And I can proudly say and go-- and how I was in the camp.
I can walk around with my head up.
And the fact is, after the war finished, people who were together with my camp in-- with me, they came up to me and said, thanks to you, I'm alive.
And that was a very good feeling.
And it still is.
They know me.
I have witnesses-- tens of them here in Toronto.
And they know-- all of them.
And my sisters were the same.
My sister Bella was the youngest kommandanten in camp.
But how she-- what she did for people was unbelievable in such conditions where we were.
And she was beaten by a-- by a meister because she didn't-- she didn't say to the workers to do the job as they did.
She picked up the-- the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't-- [LAUGHS] --she picked up the-- the ammunition from the floor.
What-- when they work on the machine, they fall down sometimes.
And this was [NON-ENGLISH], doing [NON-ENGLISH].
This is a-- this is a terrible thing.
So she did it for-- for them.
She couldn't there by all the people.
And she-- and she couldn't meister with them to do this or do that.
So she was beaten for that.
They did very bad thing.
First of all, this was a terrible thing to sell-- to sell their food.
And people were starving.
They looked like [NON-ENGLISH], like Muselmanns, they called them.
And they were selling people's shoe-- people's life!
This was one of things.
And then they took money for doing favors to them.
Favors?
When one-- one was feeling bad, to stay in the barracks.
And that was no good too, because there was-- they came.
And a-- and a company from the German see who was in the barrack.
And if you were sick, you are no good to work.
So they took them out and-- and to shoot them too!
That was no good to do that.
But they paid money for them to do that, or not to work so hard, or whatever.
And they beat them.
And this was awful-- awful what I saw-- what's-- what's going on.
And where-- where the-- the girls, were once-- once I remember came a-- a commission-- a commission or a-- a delegation.
We did not know for what it is.
And the-- the meister said there will be a delegation.
You should-- you should come out in your best.
Look good.
And-- and dress as-- as much as you have to-- to come out and look good.
OK.
So we did.
And we stayed in line.
And the-- and the commission looked at us-- how we look.
And he says, [NON-ENGLISH].
Come on out!
Sisters [NON-ENGLISH], come out!
And he said to the commission, look how they look.
And we were young and had our clothes still from home-- looked not bad-- had-- had our-- had our health.
So this was-- we were an example how the camp is running and how the camp's inmates look.
So it wasn't so bad.
The commission saw the example.
OK?
Then after that, we-- we realized what the-- who the commission was and why they took us as an example.
So the Red Cross didn't have anything.
This was a commission from the Red Cross.
And the commission didn't say anything.
So it's not bad in there.
[NON-ENGLISH].
They save my hair.
My sister had long, blonde hair.
They saved hers too.
And she washed it in black coffee.
We had black, plain coffee.
So there was no hot water.
She washed it.
And they saved her hair.
She had a-- a beautiful head of blonde hair.
And she was a beautiful girl.
Sexually?
There were.
They were.
One was-- one girl-- this is-- when I was in the camp, I heard about it.
I-- and she was from the-- from Suchedniow.
And she was a beautiful, young girl-- dressed beautifully.
Maybe she shouldn't have gone out in such a beautiful robe.
She went out to the wash room to do something.
And one German saw her, and he took-- he called her up.
And they brought-- they had sex, probably.
Then he shot her.
Then he shot her.
And we knew about that incident.
No.
Because he always had something to say about her-- that she was wrong.
All the Jews were wrong sometimes.
They didn't say any-- no punishment unless they knew what's going on.
No, there were Ukraine-- Ukrainians.
They were guards too.
They were awful.
People who wanted to escape-- they took-- they talked to that Ukrainian guard.
And they said they will give him some money.
And they should let him go through the gates.
So they-- he did.
He did let them go.
He took the money.
And then-- [SIGHS] --then he-- said it to the Germans.
He snitched on them.
And the Germans sent out right away soldiers-- not soldiers, but the-- the guards, the German guards.
And they brought him back to camp.
And then all from-- all the people from the barracks had to go and come out in the middle of the-- in a circle.
And they hung them.
That's the first time I saw.
That was a-- a lovely couple-- a man and a wife-- husband and wife.
Pardon?
Oh, it was a horrible thing to see.
We couldn't forget it.
Even now, as long as I'll live, I'll never forget that-- how they-- how they knelt beside each other.
And then they put on this-- they hung them in our eyes.
And this was not once, but a few times I heard about.
But I didn't see them hanging.
They shot them.
People escaped.
And they brought them back.
Appells?
Yeah.
In the mornings when we went to work, we had to stay in line-- four in a-- in a-- in a row.
And they-- they-- they counted us-- not appell.
They counted us.
I was in front of them.
I was in front of them seeing that the rows are OK, that they go in OK.
And this-- many, many instances I had that I-- I saved many lives.
There were selections all the time.
From time to time, were selections.
They selected people.
So they said, OK, today we have a selection.
Everybody has to come out from the barracks.
And they chose people who were very thin, not dressed.
And they were looking like Muselmanns.
And-- [SIGHS] I went there.
I saw it-- what's going on.
And I saw some girls I knew closer.
And I went to these people, to the Germans, and I spoke to them.
And I said, Herr Meister, she's a good arbeiteren.
And she works good.
And don't do-- take her out to the selection.
I'll get her some clothes from the magazine.
We had some clothes in the magazines from the people, from Treblinka-- was sent to us, to the camp.
So I said, don't do it to her.
Don't-- don't take her away.
You'll see.
I-- I guarantee you, she will look different tomorrow.
She's-- she's healthy.
She's looking not bad.
Because she's dressed like that, she doesn't have anything good-- but she works good.
And she let her go.
So there were a few.
One girl, I said, now you go to the washroom.
And I saved her life.
And they come now.
I forgot these things.
They come now and tell me that.
And this is my pleasure to hear.
This is all I've-- all my life I tried to do my best for them.
Yeah.
They're not far away.
It was a little wood-- a forest.
They took them there.
And we had heard-- we could hear the shots.
People didn't have what to eat.
They were-- and they were terribly looking-- very thin.
So that's what happened.
No.
Young children was one little guy, maybe he had seven or eight years.
He came with his parents.
And he worked in a good place where the meister wasn't so bad.
And he took him under his-- his wings.
And he looked after him.
He was very lucky.
And he survived.
Pardon me?
About seven or eight.
I don't know if he was 10 years old.
No.
Yes, I-- I-- I know his name.
And I meet him here sometimes-- [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the only child I remember.
And he was so lucky.
He was like-- he was--
--this was the last name.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And he was like the-- a-- a sheep boy.
They told them where to go, what to do, when it was messages-- a message boy-- this little guy.
And his parents were working there too.
The parents survived the war-- no, the parents did not survive.
But his brother-- no one survived, because they-- they ran away.
They ran away, and they caught them.
